note_data.title: Geometry
keyPoints: Natural numbers are all the positive integers from 1 to infinity, fractions or decimals are not included
Whole numbers are all the positive integers including zero
Integers include positive numbers, negative numbers or zero and are the most common word used by the examiner
Prime numbers are numbers that have exactly 2 factors: 1 and the number itself
Composite numbers are numbers that have more than two factors
Rational numbers are any whole number that can be written as a pure fraction or decimal, they must either end (terminating decimals) or repeat (recurring decimals)
Irrational numbers are any number that cannot be expressed in the form of a fraction, such as π, √2, √7
Real numbers are all the numbers that exist, these include all rational and irrational numbers
Squared numbers are always positive, while cube numbers may be positive or negative
Candidates are required to memorise square numbers up till 15 for Paper 1

definitions.definition: Numbers that have exactly 2 factors: 1 and the number itself
The largest number that is a common factor of two different numbers
The smallest number that is the multiple of two different numbers
An organised, unique collection of elements where you are not allowed to repeat the same element
The number obtained by interchanging the numerators and denominators of the original number

howToAnswer.instruction: Use ruler and compasses only. Show all construction arcs clearly. Do not erase construction lines.
Show all working steps. State the theorem or property used. Give answer to required accuracy.
Give reasons for each step using angle properties, theorems or given information. Use formal geometric language.

modelAnswer.question: Triangle ABC is similar to triangle PQR. AB = 6 cm, BC = 8 cm and PQ = 9 cm. Calculate the area of triangle PQR if the area of triangle ABC is 24 cm².
modelAnswer.answer: Scale factor = PQ/AB = 9/6 = 1.5. Area scale factor = (1.5)² = 2.25. Area of triangle PQR = 24 × 2.25 = 54 cm²
markBreakdown: Mark 2: Square the scale factor to get area scale factor = 2.25
Mark 3: Multiply original area by area scale factor = 54 cm²

commonMistakes: Including fractions or decimals in natural numbers when they are not included
Forgetting that integers is the most common word used by the examiner
Not recognising that squared numbers are always positive while cube numbers may be negative
Repeating the same element in a set when this is not allowed
examinerTips: Integers is the most common word used by the examiner
Candidates are required to memorise square numbers up till 15 for Paper 1
note_data.keyDiagramDescription: Circle with centre O, showing tangent from external point, radius to point of contact (perpendicular to tangent), and cyclic quadrilateral inscribed in circle
pastPaperRefs: Circle geometry was tested in M/J 2023 P2 Q7 (4 marks) — they asked students to find angle BOC using the theorem that the angle at the centre is twice the angle at the circumference
Angle calculations appeared in O/N 2022 P1 Q15 (3 marks) — candidates had to find angle ABC where the angle was subtended by the same arc as a given angle
Circle theorems were examined in M/J 2022 P2 Q8 (5 marks) — students needed to find angle BDC using the alternate segment theorem
Properties of circles featured in O/N 2023 P1 Q12 (3 marks) — requiring identification of angles in the same segment being equal
Complex circle geometry appeared in M/J 2021 P2 Q9 (6 marks) — combining multiple circle theorems to find unknown angles in cyclic quadrilaterals

workedExample.question: In the diagram, O is the centre of the circle. Angle BAC = 35°. Find angle BOC. (3 marks)
steps: Step 1: Identify that angle BAC is an angle at the circumference and angle BOC is an angle at the centre
Step 2: Recognize that both angles are subtended by the same arc BC
Step 3: Apply the theorem: angle at centre = 2 × angle at circumference
workedExample.markScheme: 1 mark for identifying the relationship, 1 mark for correct theorem application, 1 mark for correct calculation

examinerWarnings: Examiner Report 2023: 'Many candidates failed to clearly state which circle theorem they were using' — always write the theorem name (e.g., 'angle at centre = 2 × angle at circumference') when solving circle geometry problems

Examiner Report 2022: 'Students often confused angles in the same segment with angles subtended by the same arc' — clearly identify whether angles are at the centre, circumference, or in alternate segments before applying theorems
Examiner Report 2023: 'Common error was not marking equal angles on diagrams' — always mark equal angles with the same symbol and label your working clearly on the diagram
note_data.keyDiagram: Draw a circle with centre O. Show a chord AB creating an arc. Mark angle AOB at the centre and angle ACB at the circumference (where C is any point on the major arc). Label the centre angle as 2x and circumference angle as x. Include arrows showing the arc AB and clearly mark the centre O. Add tangent lines where relevant showing the 90° angle with radius.
note_data.quickRecap: The number family includes natural numbers (positive integers from 1 to infinity), whole numbers (positive integers including zero), and integers (positive, negative numbers or zero). Prime numbers have exactly 2 factors while composite numbers have more than two factors. Numbers are classified as rational (can be expressed as fractions) or irrational (cannot be expressed as fractions), with all numbers together forming real numbers.
